issue_title: remote git ignored files can't be added on travis
issue_body:
doctor fails on travis, presumably because some files in the docs are git ignored and can't be added

```
Switched to a new branch 'gh-pages'

The following paths are ignored by one of your .gitignore files:

examples/markov_chain.hires.png

Use -f if you really want to add them.

...

git add ./examples/markov_chain.hires.png
```
and that command fails, so the build stop. [Full log](https://travis-ci.org/fredRos/pypmc/jobs/279533914)

I checked on my desktop why this file is ignored with `git check-ignore -v doc/_build/examples/markov_chain.hires.png` and removed the corresponding line from the `.gitignore`. I can locally add the `png` w/o error. The build on travis still [fails](https://travis-ci.org/fredRos/pypmc/jobs/279838792). As before, it warns about the `png` file but stops at a different position

```
The following paths are ignored by one of your .gitignore files:

examples/markov_chain.hires.png

Use -f if you really want to add them.

Setting git attributes

git config --global user.name 'Doctr (Travis CI)'

git config --global u
```

Finally I removed the `.gitignore` altogether but the build still failed with the same output. After some trying around, I thought it must be due to the remote `gh-pages` branch on github, so I removed that and just triggered a rebuild on travis and finally the deployment succeeded. I think I initially created a `gh-pages` by cloning the master branch but I'm not sure. 

This ignore-file problem was quite a stumbling block for me. Perhaps you can update doctr or its docs to indicate what to do in this situation.

issue_title: Reduce Travis and Appveyor CIs to process just the PR
issue_body:
Right now, the web hooks for Travis and Appveyor are configured to invoke them for both the PR code and for the code when merged. This runs them twice as often without much of a benefit, and causes the elapsed time for the checks to be twice as long.

It seems that many other projects on GitHub that use Travis and Appveyor just run them on the PR code, for example [pip](https://github.com/pypa/pip) or [click-spinner](https://github.com/click-contrib/click-spinner).

Don't know exactly which triggers need to be disabled, but in the PR check list, this must look as follows after reducing the configuration:
```
    continuous-integration/appveyor/pr — AppVeyor build succeeded
    continuous-integration/travis-ci/pr — The Travis CI build passed
```

issue_title: Actor methods should do some checking when they are invoked instead of when they are executed.
issue_body:
Currently, if we do

```python
import ray

ray.init()

@ray.actor
class Foo(object):
  def __init__(self):
    pass
  def get_val(self, x):

f = Foo()
```

import ray

f.remote()

issue_title: Discussion about job management agent
issue_body:
Hi,

There has been a lot of discussions on job submission and we're about to work on it.

Right now we're thinking about implementing the job submission with a single http portal, plus a job management agent which runs on each machine in the cluster.  

When user submits a job, http server upload the files (java jars, python scripts) for this job to a place, and write the location to GCS, and the agent running on each machine would then download the files to its local drive, and communicate with local node manager when it's ready.

The goal for the agent is to:
- download submitted job files to local drive, and communicate with Node manager on whether it's ready to execute tasks for a particular job. Java jar files can be very large, e.g. a few hundred megabytes.
- manage all the job files on local machine.
- manage sharing same libraries among different jobs.


I'd like to seek your comments on this. Do you think we need a separate agent for this, or do we want to put this functionality to node manager?

Thanks!

issue_title: Writing dynamic (form-agnostic) layout objects
issue_body:
I've found the following line in [templatetags/crispy_forms_tags.py#L132](https://github.com/maraujop/django-crispy-forms/blob/dev/crispy_forms/templatetags/crispy_forms_tags.py#L132):

```
# If we have a helper's layout we use it, for the form or the formset's forms
if helper and helper.layout:
```

Shouldn't that be as follows instead?

```
if helper is not None and helper.layout is not None:
```

The reason is that right now, the truthyness check is based on the `__len__` methods implemented by `LayoutObject` (thereby False if it has no fields) and `FormHelper`'s base class `DynamicLayoutHandler` (thereby False if it either has no layout object or that layout object has no fields).
This is because Python uses [__len__ in absence of __nonzero__ to determine truthyness](https://docs.python.org/2/library/stdtypes.html#truth-value-testing).

The reason for this suggested change is that I'd like to write a layout object which is form-agnostic, i.e. it applies a certain behavior dynamically to any form it gets thrown at. Right now, this doesn't appear to be easily possible.

Would you agree that this change expresses the intention of that line better? I don't see why that check would be based on the existence of fields.

I believe this expression is also related to the documentation stating the following in the [Helper attributes you can set](http://django-crispy-forms.readthedocs.io/en/latest/form_helper.html#helper-attributes-you-can-set):

> field_template
> When set allows you to render a form/formset using a custom field template. Default template is at {{ TEMPLATE_PACK }}/field.html. Beware that this is only effective when setting a FormHelper.layout.

When initially reading this, I didn't understand why a layout is necessary to make the `field_template` take effect. I'd like to be able to set a `field_template` without having to write my own layout object.

For that, I'm using the following workaround subclasses for now:

```
class NoopLayout(Layout):
    """
    A special layout manager for crispy, used to unlock some functionality in crispy (like custom field templates),
    which are only available when a layout manager is used, even though none is needed.

    So this layout manager just pretends to know how to lay out any form, but then when it is asked to lay out an
    actual form, it will just return the fields in the order from the form.
    """
    def __nonzero__(self):
        """ This is just to convince the crispy template tag that we're a valid layout object, which knows
            about fields (even though we have no clue yet!).
        """
        return True

    def render(self, form, *args, **kwargs):
        # now we know the actual form fields!
        self.fields = form.fields.keys()

        # [TODO: we could do something with this specific form's fields here]

        # let the base class handle the actual rendering of fields into HTML
        return super(NoopLayout, self).render(form, *args, **kwargs)

class LazyLayoutFormHelper(FormHelper):
    """
    A simply FormHelper subclass which changes behavior of the base class in only one aspect: the original
    FormHelper is truthy if and exactly if it has a layout object and that layout object has at least one field.
    This is due to its base class DynamicLayoutHandler overriding __len__, which is Python uses in absence of
    a __nonzero__ implementation to determine truthyness.

    A LazyLayoutFormHelper however is always truthy, no matter whether it has a layout object and how many
    fields that layout object has. Thereby it always passes the crispy template tags helper existence checks.
    """
    def __nonzero__(self):
        return True
```

This allows to write a form helper like this, which can be used with any simple Django form, like the `django.contrib.auth` forms (haven't tried any more complex ones):

```
my_helper = LazyLayoutFormHelper()   # we need a special FormHelper when using NoopLayout, because otherwise crispy will believe our layout object is invalid
my_helper.layout = NoopLayout()   # field_template has no effect unless a custom layout object is used
my_helper.field_template = 'my-custom-field.html'
```

issue_title: Error in documentation
issue_body:
The install document states when you want django_helpdesk to be at the root of the url patterns to use

`url(r'', include('helpdesk.urls', namespace='helpdesk')),`
In django 1.10.5 This does not work and produces a reverse lookup failure for helpdesk_home.
Changing this to 
`url(r'', include('helpdesk.urls')),`
solves the issue.

issue_title: [V3 Core] Increase the maximum amount of characters in a single help message
issue_body:
### Type:

- [X] Suggestion
- [ ] Bug

### Brief description of the problem
Currently, the maximum amount of characters allowed in all fields combined in a single help embed is 1000. [See this code](https://github.com/Cog-Creators/Red-DiscordBot/blob/V3/develop/redbot/core/help_formatter.py#L193).

I think this is way too little and causes the bot to split `[p]help` into multiple messages much too quickly. Instead, I think the limit should be increased to something like 2000. 
Technically, the maximum amount of characters a rich embed can have is 6000, but I can understand that it's not desirable for the embeds to be *that* big (especially when taking mobile users into consideration).
On the contrary, I think a limit of 1000 makes the bot unnecessarily split the fields too quickly. 2000 is a nice middle ground between the two.

TL;DR - Increase the limit of 1000 chars per help embed to something like 2000 chars per help embed.

issue_title: Use introspection to combine `capture` and `acapture`
issue_body:

I don't necessarily endorse doing so but thought I'd bring it up anyway.

issue_title: Support appropriate matching between task requirements and available resources
issue_body:
Currently, @djf604 has workflows where different apps have different resource requirements. This is currently leading to inefficiencies in his workflow due to non-optimal task packing. For running Parsl at large scales, I think it is critical that we address this problem. Clearly this is a complicated thing to tackle, with reams of papers written about it. I think there are two main options:

1. Avoid re-inventing the wheel and solving this problem ourselves; one possibility @yadudoc  suggested is to allow users to plug Parsl into [Work Queue](http://ccl.cse.nd.edu/software/workqueue/) or other software which already addresses this.
2. Implement in Parsl.

Thoughts?

issue_title: Error in setInteger method (FMI2)
issue_body:
The method setInteger (FMI2) contains the implementation of setString:

# fmi2.py - v0.0.8.
```python
def setInteger(self, vr, value):
    value = map(lambda s: s.encode('utf-8'), value)
    value = (fmi2String * len(vr))(*value)
    self.fmi2SetString(self.component, vr, len(vr), value)
```

# fmi2.py - correction
```python
def setInteger(self, vr, value):
    vr = (fmi2ValueReference * len(vr))(*vr)
    value = (fmi2Integer * len(vr))(*value)
    self.fmi2SetInteger(self.component, vr, len(vr), value) 
```

issue_title: thread.error: can't start new thread on UnivaGridEngine
issue_body:
Toil failed when running on UnivaGridEngine cluster environment.

## Environments

```

## Error details

When I run the [quick start](http://toil.readthedocs.io/en/3.15.0/gettingStarted/quickStart.html) on the official document with `python helloWorld.py bucket`, it returned an error.

```
Traceback (most recent call last):

## `--gridOption gridEngine`  ignores maxCores option

When I run  `python helloWorld.py bucket --batchSystem gridEngine --maxCores 1`, the same error `thread.error: can't start new thread` occurred. issue_title: Add path formatting flags
issue_body:
Requesting endpoints that contain hyphens is currently not very straight-forward nor user-friendly. A new `hyphenated` option could be added to the wrapper. When enabled, underscores in the attribute names will be replaced with hyphens when forming the URL:
```python
# before
api('hyphenated-endpoint').get()

# after
api = tortilla.wrap('https://api.example.org', hyphenated=True)
api.hyphenated_endpoint.get()  # GET https://api.example.org/hyphenated-endpoint
```

Other option flags, such as `camelcased`, could be implemented as well to make code more PEP-8 compliant:
```python
# before
api.camelCasedEndpoint.get()

# after
api = tortilla.wrap('https://api.example.org', camelcased=True)
api.camel_cased_endpoint.get()  # GET https://api.example.org/camelCasedEndpoint
```

I think that this formatting should only happen in `__getattr__` to keep `__call__` open for potential special situations.

issue_title: Usage of assert statements in optimized mode
issue_body:
I've noticed that Lark uses assert statements in some places outside of tests. Just by doing `git grep assert` in the lark directory we can see a big list of these.

Assert statements are ignored when Python is running in optimized mode. Optimize mode is used in some projects when Python is deployed and/or distributed. I did a quick experiment, and running the following like of code fails in both normal and optimized mode:

```
lark.Lark(grammar='start: "my_token"', parser='not_a_parser')
```

But the yielded exception is different. Normal mode is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 108, in __init__
    self.options = LarkOptions(options)
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 68, in __init__
    assert self.parser in ('earley', 'lalr', 'cyk', None)
AssertionError
```

And optimized is:

```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module>
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 165, in __init__
    self.parser = self._build_parser()
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/lark.py", line 177, in _build_parser
    self.parser_class = get_frontend(self.options.parser, self.options.lexer)
  File "/home/my_user/opensource/lark/lark/parser_frontends.py", line 187, in get_frontend
    raise ValueError('Unknown parser: %s' % parser)
ValueError: Unknown parser: not_a_parser
```

This particular example might not be too problematic, but might the usage of asserts became problematic for people running lark in optimized mode?

issue_title: Replacing Simpson's rule integration from SBProfile Simpson.h
issue_body: Currently Simpson.h has a NR-based routine for integration by Simpson's rule, which Gary modified so it's written as a template with the integrand as the template argument.  We should replace this with some alternative, such as Mike's integration package.  Gary points out the decision to be made: whether to adopt the templating interface, or to change sbprofile to do more traditional function calls.  The advantage of the template is that the integrand can be a class instead of a function, which allows better control of internal variables.
